pair_convention.spot_explanation: SGD/CHF at 0.6135 means 1 SGD buys 0.6135 CHF. A rising SGD/CHF rate means SGD is strengthening against CHF (each SGD buys more CHF), while a falling rate means SGD is weakening against CHF (each SGD buys fewer CHF). The pair is currently near the 15th percentile of its 52-week range, indicating SGD is trading close to its weakest levels versus CHF over the past year.

executive_summary: SGD/CHF trades at 0.6135 (May 9 close), sitting in the 15th percentile of its 52-week range after declining approximately 2.3% over the past year and 8.5% from the March 2025 high of 0.6700. The pair is caught between two competing forces: both Singapore and Switzerland possess AAA sovereign ratings, massive current account surpluses, and managed-currency regimes that structurally support their respective currencies. The dominant bilateral narrative is that MAS's April 2026 slope tightening — the first since 2022 — provides a fresh hawkish impulse for SGD, while the SNB remains anchored at 0.00% with intervention rhetoric capping CHF strength. The key bilateral differential favouring SGD/CHF upside is the +115bps carry advantage (SORA ~1.15% vs SNB 0.00%) combined with Singapore's dramatically superior growth profile (+4.6% vs +0.8% y/y GDP). Both dossiers are bullish their respective currencies, but the SGD bullish case (conviction 3/5 tactical, 4/5 strategic) is materially stronger than the CHF bullish case (conviction 2/5 tactical, 3/5 strategic), creating a net pair signal of SGD outperformance. Our stance is tactically Bullish at conviction 3/5 and strategically Bullish at conviction 3/5, reflecting the view that Singapore's AI-electronics growth tailwind, MAS tightening bias, and positive carry will gradually push the pair higher, though the magnitude is constrained by CHF's safe-haven demand in the ongoing Iran ceasefire fragility and the SNB's deep structural surplus that mirrors Singapore's own.

market_context.valuation_assessment: On a PPP basis, the SGD dossier shows SGD approximately 24% undervalued versus CHF, which is the largest PPP deviation in the SGD cross set. The CHF dossier corroborates this, noting CHF 3-11% overvalued versus SGD — the smallest overvaluation gap among CHF crosses. This suggests SGD/CHF has substantial structural upside over multi-year horizons but PPP gaps can persist for extended periods. The pair's current level around 0.6135 is roughly 8% below the pre-Iran-war level of ~0.6680, suggesting CHF's safe-haven premium has compressed the pair meaningfully.

positioning.net_pair_implication: There is no leveraged SGD position to unwind, while the CHF asset-manager short is extreme at the 95th percentile of 5-year history. An Iran re-escalation triggering CHF short-covering would push SGD/CHF lower (bearish for the pair), while a durable ceasefire validating existing shorts would support SGD/CHF recovery (bullish). The asymmetry creates two-way binary risk for the pair.
positioning.crowding_assessment: CHF net short extreme (95th percentile); SGD positioning uninformative. Pair-level crowding is driven by CHF dynamics only.

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50d SMA at 0.6170, 100d SMA at 0.6140, 200d SMA at 0.6154 — all within a narrow ~30-pip cluster; spot at 0.6135 trades below all three, indicating consolidation regime rather than a trending market
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Low-volatility range consolidation near cycle lows; pair has failed to sustain gains above 0.6200 since the March 31 local high of 0.6224, with most recent closes clustered in the 0.6130-0.6160 area. MA tangle and tightening range signal a breakout pending.

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: IV (~5.5-6.5%) exceeds RV (~4.5-5.0%), embedding a small event premium for SNB June 18 and MAS July 14 meetings; variance risk premium is positive (implied > realised), modestly expensive for option buyers
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: Mild SGD/CHF put bid implied by the broader CHF call-bid environment in risk-reversals; magnitude small given both currencies' safe-haven characteristics limiting skew
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Flat to mildly backwardated given the cluster of central bank events in June-July 2026 (SNB June 18, MAS July 14); near-term event premium keeps front-end IV elevated relative to back end
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Low vol / consolidating — daily ranges average 15-20 pips over past 30 sessions; consistent with managed-float nature of both currencies limiting daily ranges

market_context.recent_regime: SGD/CHF has been range-bound in the 0.6120-0.6250 zone since late March 2026, after stabilising from the sharp Iran-war-driven selloff that took the pair from 0.6680 to 0.6033 between March and late January 2026. The recent 20 candles show a tightening range with a slight downward drift — the pair made a local high of 0.6224 on March 31 but has failed to sustain gains above 0.6200, with most recent closes clustered in the 0.6130-0.6160 area. The dominant regime is low-volatility consolidation near cycle lows, with neither currency generating enough momentum to break the range decisively.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately 35 pips (0.57%) against before carry is erased over 6M; on a 1-month basis, SGD/CHF needs to fall only ~10 pips per month to erode the carry advantage

forward_points.1m: +6 pips (SGD at premium, reflecting positive rate differential)

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The carry differential is unambiguously SGD-positive. Singapore's SORA 3M Compounded trades at approximately 1.15%, while the SNB policy rate sits at 0.00%, creating an annualised carry of +115bps in favour of long SGD/CHF. At estimated pair 3M IV of ~6%, the carry-to-vol ratio is approximately 0.19 — modest but positive, making this one of only two carry-positive CHF expressions in G10 (alongside JPY/CHF). On a 1-month holding period, the carry accrual is approximately +10 pips (0.6135 × 115bps / 12 = ~5.9 pips/month). The forward curve reflects this: 1M forward points are approximately +6 pips, 3M approximately +17 pips, and 6M approximately +35 pips — all embedding the rate differential in SGD's favour. The carry regime is working modestly for this pair: over the past 3 months, SGD/CHF has gained 0.37% while earning approximately 29bps of carry, suggesting spot appreciation has added to carry returns. The breakeven move over 6 months is approximately 35 pips of SGD/CHF depreciation — a relatively low hurdle given the pair's 52-week range of ~667 pips.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral policy differential is the clearest directional signal in this pair. MAS tightened the S$NEER slope on April 14, 2026 — the first tightening since 2022 — while the SNB held at 0.00% on March 19 with escalated intervention rhetoric but no rate action. This creates a widening policy divergence: MAS is actively tightening while the SNB is constrained at the zero lower bound with a high bar for negative rates. The +115bps carry differential (SORA 1.15% vs SNB 0.00%) is the largest positive carry SGD enjoys against any G10 currency except JPY. The tactical signal is reinforced by the meeting calendar: MAS has no scheduled communication for 2-4 weeks (next MPS July 14), while the SNB meets June 18 where the consensus is a hold at 0.00% — meaning the policy status quo of SGD-hawkish / CHF-anchored persists through the tactical window. Singapore's 2Y yield at ~1.65% versus Swiss 2Y at ~0.10% creates a 155bps short-end spread that supports SGD/CHF through the forward curve. The transmission mechanism is via the S$NEER appreciation slope, which mechanically delivers continued SGD strength against all basket currencies including CHF.
key_bilateral_metrics: SORA 3M: 1.15% vs SNB rate: 0.00% = +115bps differential
SGS 2Y: ~1.65% vs Swiss 2Y: ~0.10% = +155bps
SGS 10Y: 2.10% vs Swiss 10Y: 0.35% = +175bps
MAS April tightening vs SNB March hold — diverging policy direction
Annualised carry: +115bps; carry-to-vol ratio: ~0.19
tactical.what_is_priced: The carry differential is well-embedded in forward points (+35 pips at 6M) but the probability of further MAS tightening at July (~25% market vs our 35%) creates a small bullish-SGD/CHF edge. An SNB cut to negative rates is priced at only 15-25% by June — if triggered, it would amplify SGD/CHF upside dramatically.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, the policy differential trajectory favours SGD/CHF widening. MAS is in tightening mode (or at minimum bias-tightened) with the next opportunity at July 14 MPS, while the SNB faces a binary: either hold at 0% with intensified intervention rhetoric (base case ~70%) or cut to -0.25% if EUR/CHF sustains below 0.90 and medium-term inflation forecast drops below 0.3% (tail ~15%). In either scenario, the SNB does not tighten — meaning the best case for CHF on rates is status quo, while the tail case is rates widening further in SGD's favour. The MAS-SNB policy reaction function asymmetry is the strategic edge: MAS reacts to above-target inflation by tightening (S$NEER slope), while the SNB reacts to below-target inflation by easing or intervening — both responses mechanically favour SGD/CHF. Over 6 months, if the Fed cuts once under Warsh, the USD weakening benefits SGD more than CHF because USD/SGD has a higher correlation to DXY than USD/CHF in the current regime.
key_bilateral_metrics: Expected MAS path: hold or further tighten (35% probability of July tightening)
Expected SNB path: hold at 0% (70%) or cut to -0.25% (15%)
6M forward-implied carry: ~+35 pips (SGD appreciation embedded)
Rate path convergence: nil — paths diverge further if MAS tightens
DBS end-2026 USD/SGD: 1.24-1.26; Goldman end-2026 USD/CHF: 0.76
strategic.what_is_priced: Forwards embed +35 pips of SGD/CHF appreciation at 6M. Our expected spot at 6M is 0.6200-0.6300, implying 65-165 pips of upside vs current spot — well above the forward-implied path. The edge is in the MAS-tightening tail (35% probability) and the SNB-cut tail (15%), both of which independently push SGD/CHF higher.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons bullish with strategic confidence marginally higher (75 vs 72) because the policy divergence compounds over time and the MAS July MPS introduces a tightening catalyst within the strategic window.

carry_analysis.pair_specific_note: One of only two carry-positive CHF expressions in G10 (alongside JPY/CHF); carry accrual of ~10 pips/month; carry compounds in SGD's favour across all tenors

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The growth differential between Singapore and Switzerland is the widest in any G10+SGD cross pair. Singapore's Q1 2026 GDP at +4.6% y/y dwarfs Switzerland's Q4 2025 GDP at +0.8% y/y (with 2026 SECO forecast at just 1.0%). The inflation picture creates an interesting bilateral dynamic: Singapore headline CPI at 1.8% (core 1.7%) is rising on energy pass-through, while Swiss headline CPI at 0.6% (core 0.3%) is uniquely bifurcated — headline rising on energy but core falling. Both central banks face imported inflation from the Iran energy shock, but the policy responses diverge: MAS tightens (bullish SGD), while the SNB watches and threatens intervention (neutral-to-bearish CHF on rates). The data surprise differential favours SGD: Singapore's March NODX at +15.3% y/y (electronics +74%) massively beat consensus (+9.4%), while Switzerland's PMI at 54.5 beat consensus (52.0) but the KOF at 97.9 remains below 100. The AI-electronics tailwind is Singapore-specific and creates a bilateral growth edge that CHF cannot match. The April CPI release for Singapore (May 25) is a tactical catalyst that could lift MAS-tightening odds if it prints ≥2.0%.
key_bilateral_metrics: GDP differential: Singapore +4.6% vs Switzerland +0.8% = +380bps
Headline CPI: Singapore 1.8% vs Switzerland 0.6% = +120bps
Core CPI: Singapore 1.7% vs Switzerland 0.3% = +140bps
PMI: Singapore 50.7 vs Switzerland 54.5 (Swiss manufacturing stronger)
NODX March +15.3% y/y; Swiss PPI/IPI -2.7% y/y (Swiss producer deflation)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced Singapore's growth advantage into the S$NEER position (upper half of band) but has not fully priced the AI-electronics export tailwind's durability. Swiss real-yield advantage (negative real rates attract safe-haven capital) partially offsets, but the growth differential is too wide to be fully neutralised.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, the growth differential narrows modestly as Singapore's Q1 base effect fades (MTI 2026 forecast 2.0-4.0% vs SECO 1.0% for Switzerland), but the gap remains substantial. The strategic risk is if Switzerland's inflation undershoots 0% and forces SNB negative rates — which would be initially bearish CHF but historically has taken years to transmit. The AI-electronics supercycle is the strategic differentiator: Singapore captures a disproportionate share of global semiconductor production and re-exports, while Switzerland's pharma-services export mix is stable but not accelerating. Strategic confidence is lower than tactical (62 vs 70) because the 6M horizon includes uncertainty around Iran energy resolution's impact on both economies — Singapore as a net oil importer suffers more from elevated oil, while Switzerland's terms of trade are less oil-sensitive.
key_bilateral_metrics: 2026 GDP forecast: Singapore 2.0-4.0% vs Switzerland 1.0%
AI-electronics export growth: Singapore-specific tailwind (+74% y/y electronics March)
Swiss pharma exports: stable ~+1.4% y/y (non-cyclical)
Singapore unemployment: 2.1% vs Switzerland 3.0%
Singapore tourism Q1: 4.4mn arrivals (structural services export)
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus expects Singapore growth to moderate toward 2.5-3.0% and Swiss growth to hold ~1.0%. We see Singapore at 3.0-3.5% on AI tailwind — a modest upside vs consensus that supports SGD/CHF at the margin.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons bullish; tactical confidence higher (70 vs 62) because near-term data releases (Singapore April CPI, NODX) are likely to come in strong, while the strategic horizon introduces Iran-resolution uncertainty affecting Singapore's energy import bill.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: SGD/CHF has elevated risk sensitivity given CHF safe-haven character vs SGD Asian beta; Iran binary amplifies the risk appetite signal for this pair specifically — weight increased +3% tactical and +3% strategic

tactical.bilateral_analysis: SGD/CHF is a moderate risk-sensitivity pair where CHF's safe-haven character dominates in risk-off events while SGD acts as a regional Asian safe haven but with lower global safe-haven beta. The pair's behaviour during the Iran war confirms this: SGD/CHF dropped approximately 10% from 0.6700 to 0.6033 as CHF surged on geopolitical fear. With the Iran ceasefire fragile (Operation Epic Fury declared concluded May 5 but Iran still reviewing the 14-point MoU), the near-term risk is for another bout of CHF safe-haven demand if negotiations break down. VIX at 17.13 is calm, but gold at $4,719/oz signals residual geopolitical premium. The pair has a negative beta to VIX (rising VIX = falling SGD/CHF) and positive correlation to equity risk-on. The tactical signal is mildly bearish for SGD/CHF because the binary Iran risk creates a fat left tail — a re-escalation could push SGD/CHF back toward 0.6000, while a durable peace would likely only support a move toward 0.6250-0.6300. The payoff distribution is asymmetrically CHF-positive on the downside.
key_bilateral_metrics: VIX: 17.13 (calm); gold: $4,719/oz (elevated geopolitical premium)
SGD/CHF beta to VIX: approximately -0.15 to -0.25
Iran ceasefire probability: 45-55% durable (our estimate)
SGD/CHF drawdown on Iran war start: ~10% (Feb-Apr 2025)
CHF safe-haven beta: ~+0.20 vs VIX; SGD safe-haven beta: ~+0.05
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices the ceasefire as broadly durable (low VIX, equities at highs). We see 25% probability of re-escalation in the next 2-4 weeks vs market-implied 10-15%, making the tail-risk asymmetry bearish for SGD/CHF.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, the risk environment is harder to call directionally because both currencies benefit from safe-haven characteristics — CHF globally, SGD regionally in Asia. In a sustained risk-off scenario (China hard landing, European fiscal stress), CHF outperforms SGD given its deeper global safe-haven status. In a risk-on scenario, SGD benefits more from Asian growth flows and AI-electronics demand. The net effect over 6 months averages toward neutral as multiple risk episodes are likely but non-directionally distributed. The pair's moderate risk sensitivity means it is less affected by global sentiment shifts than AUD/CHF or NZD/CHF.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M tail-risk event probability: ~50% for at least one major risk-off event
SGD/CHF response to typical risk-off event: -1.5% to -3.0%
SGD/CHF response to typical risk-on rally: +1.0% to +2.0%
Both currencies classified as safe-haven (CHF > SGD on global scale)
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic options price balanced risks. The pair's moderate risk sensitivity means the risk-appetite factor generates limited directional alpha over 6 months.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bearish (60) / Strategic Neutral (55) — the divergence reflects the binary Iran ceasefire risk dominating the 2-4 week window, while the 6M horizon averages across multiple possible risk events with offsetting safe-haven dynamics.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Neither currency has informative CFTC futures positioning (SGD has zero open interest; CHF has extreme asset-manager net-short at -37,534 contracts). For the pair, the CHF short could amplify SGD/CHF downside on a risk-off squeeze (asset managers cover CHF shorts → CHF rises → SGD/CHF falls). Conversely, on risk-on, the CHF short is validated and SGD/CHF rises. MAS reserves growth (+USD 8bn in April) signals MAS dampening SGD appreciation — a contrarian signal suggesting S$NEER is near the band ceiling, capping near-term SGD/CHF upside via the S$NEER channel. The bilateral flow story is unique: Swiss direct investment in Singapore was approximately CHF 63 billion in 2023, higher than Swiss investments in China, India, Japan and South Korea combined. Over 400 Swiss companies have operations in Singapore, creating structural CHF-to-SGD conversion flows for operational expenses. This bilateral investment linkage provides a slow-burn structural SGD bid from Swiss corporate flows, though the magnitude is too small to dominate spot dynamics over 2-4 weeks.
key_bilateral_metrics: CFTC SGD: zero open interest (uninformative)
CFTC CHF asset managers: -37,534 contracts (95th percentile, extreme short)
MAS reserves: +USD 8bn April (dampening SGD appreciation)
Swiss FDI in Singapore: ~CHF 63bn (2023)
400+ Swiss companies in Singapore (operational SGD demand)
tactical.what_is_priced: Positioning creates no clear directional signal for SGD/CHF tactically. The CHF short could go either way depending on Iran resolution — if ceasefire holds, shorts validated (pair rises); if ceasefire breaks, shorts squeezed (pair falls). Net: neutral.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, the structural flow story favouring SGD/CHF becomes more relevant. Singapore's family office inflows, AI-export repatriation flows, and the Swiss FDI operational flows all generate persistent SGD demand. Switzerland's 7% current account surplus generates structural CHF demand via a different channel (goods/services/primary income), but this flow is non-bilateral. The EFTA-Singapore Free Trade Agreement entered into force in 2003, and in September 2025 the parties signed a Digital Economy Agreement, deepening the institutional framework for bilateral flows. A Declaration of Enhanced Partnership signed in 2014 provides for regular political dialogue and closer cooperation in economic, financial, research and innovation fields. Over 6 months, the CHF asset-manager net-short may partially normalise (slow unwind), which would reduce the squeeze tailwind but also reduce CHF demand — net effect muted. Strategic positioning favours SGD/CHF modestly via the structural bilateral investment channel.
key_bilateral_metrics: EFTA-Singapore FTA: in force since 2003; DEA signed September 2025
Swiss FDI in Singapore: ~CHF 63bn (structural; expected to grow)
Singapore family office flows: 2,000+ licensed (Greater China wealth migration)
MAS reserves trajectory: USD 60-80bn annualised accumulation
strategic.what_is_priced: The market underweights bilateral Switzerland-Singapore investment flows as a structural SGD positive. The flow magnitude is modest relative to both currencies' total turnover but directionally supportive.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (50) / Strategic Bullish (55) — the divergence reflects the binary CHF-positioning risk in the near term being replaced by structural flow dynamics over 6 months.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: CHF asset-manager short extreme (-37,534 contracts, 95th percentile); SGD positioning uninformative (zero CFTC open interest). Net pair positioning creates binary two-way risk driven entirely by CHF short dynamics.
tactical.unwind_trigger: Iran re-escalation with Brent sustaining above $110/bbl triggering CHF safe-haven short-covering; VIX spiking above high-20s would force mechanical squeeze of CHF shorts, pushing SGD/CHF lower
tactical.historical_analogue: CHF short-squeeze events in 2015 (SNB floor removal) and 2022 (Ukraine war) demonstrated that extreme CHF short positioning can unwind violently; for SGD/CHF specifically, the Iran war onset in early 2025 caused a ~10% drawdown from 0.6700 to 0.6033 as CHF shorts were covered
tactical.contrarian_case: The extreme CHF asset-manager short (-37,534 contracts, 95th percentile of 5-year history) creates a substantial squeeze risk. If the Iran ceasefire collapses or a new geopolitical shock emerges, CHF short-covering could overwhelm the +115bps carry advantage and push SGD/CHF through support levels rapidly. The crowded short creates a non-linear downside scenario where even a modest risk-off catalyst generates outsized CHF appreciation. Position sizing for long SGD/CHF must account for this tail risk.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: Over 6 months, CHF net-short likely to partially normalise by 15-20k contracts through slow unwind as carry trades are re-assessed. SGD positioning remains uninformative. Net effect: gradual reduction in CHF-squeeze tail risk, allowing fundamental SGD/CHF upside to reassert.
strategic.unwind_trigger: Sustained Iran re-escalation over 4+ weeks forcing systematic deleveraging of CHF carry trades; alternatively, a new global safe-haven event (Taiwan-China escalation, European banking stress) restarting CHF short-covering cycles
strategic.historical_analogue: Post-2019 CHF safe-haven demand cycles showed gradual normalisation of positioning over 3-6 months once risk events faded; the 2023 banking stress episode saw CHF shorts rebuild after initial covering, providing structural SGD/CHF support once the positioning normalised
strategic.contrarian_case: Over 6 months, if the CHF asset-manager short extends further (new carry trades established against CHF as SNB holds at zero) rather than normalising, SGD/CHF could face renewed squeeze risk from any subsequent risk-off episode. The structural CHF short acts as an ongoing overhang that periodically disrupts the fundamental SGD/CHF uptrend, and the slow unwind scenario requires sustained risk-on conditions to materialise.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both Singapore and Switzerland run massive current account surpluses — Singapore at 17.5% of GDP and Switzerland at ~7% of GDP. The net effect for the pair depends on the CHANGE in external balance, not the level. The Iran energy shock hits Singapore harder as a net oil importer — Singapore's terms of trade deteriorate on higher oil, while Switzerland's pharma-services-dominated export mix is less oil-sensitive (though Switzerland also imports energy). The offset is Singapore's refining margins, which surge when crude prices spike, and the AI-electronics export boom (+74% y/y March NODX) which more than compensates. Using the commodity direction rules: Oil ↑ → mildly negative Singapore (net importer) vs neutral Switzerland (pharma-services dominant) = marginally bearish SGD/CHF. Gold ↑ → positive for Switzerland (65-70% of global gold refined in Switzerland) vs neutral SGD = bearish SGD/CHF. Net commodity exposure: the current elevated oil + gold environment marginally favours CHF over SGD, making the tactical terms-of-trade signal neutral-to-slightly-bearish for the pair.
key_bilateral_metrics: Singapore C/A: 17.5% of GDP vs Switzerland C/A: ~7% of GDP
Oil sensitivity: Singapore net importer; Switzerland mildly net importer
Gold trade: Switzerland refines 65-70% of global gold; gold at $4,719
Singapore NODX electronics: +74% y/y (offsets energy import cost)
Swiss trade balance Feb: +CHF 4.4bn
tactical.what_is_priced: The energy-import cost is priced into both currencies. The gold-trade tailwind for CHF from $4,700+ gold prices is underestimated relative to its impact on Swiss trade flows. Net tactical signal: neutral.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, Singapore's structural current account surplus advantage (17.5% vs 7%) dominates. The absolute magnitude of Singapore's surplus generates approximately SGD 5-6bn of monthly structural currency demand — equivalent to ~10% of SGD M2 — while Switzerland's surplus generates ~CHF 5-6bn monthly. On a GDP-normalised basis, Singapore's surplus is 2.5x Switzerland's, creating a stronger structural support for SGD. The AI-electronics tailwind is the bilateral differentiator: Singapore's export mix is accelerating while Switzerland's pharma exports are stable. If oil normalises to $80-90 by Q3 (base case 50% probability), Singapore's terms-of-trade headwind recedes and the pair's C/A differential reasserts cleanly. The bilateral trade channel is also relevant: trade between the two countries has grown substantially since the 2003 EFTA-Singapore FTA.
key_bilateral_metrics: Singapore C/A: 17.5% of GDP (largest in G10+SGD set)
Switzerland C/A: ~7% of GDP (2nd-3rd largest in G10+CHF set)
C/A differential: +10.5 percentage points in SGD's favour
Singapore AI-electronics export growth: durable +12% y/y expected through year-end
Switzerland pharma exports: stable +1-2% y/y
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices both surpluses as structural CHF/SGD support but underweights the Singapore C/A's growth trajectory (AI-electronics acceleration) relative to Switzerland's steady-state pharma surplus.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (55) / Strategic Bullish (65) — the divergence is the classic terms-of-trade timing: near-term oil/gold dynamics marginally favour CHF, while the structural C/A differential + AI-electronics tailwind dominate over 6 months.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: Singapore: net oil importer (terms of trade deteriorate when oil rises); major refining hub (refining margins rise with crude spreads, partial offset); gold-neutral; electronics/semiconductors export-driven (non-commodity)
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: Switzerland: refines 65-70% of global gold (gold price appreciation = positive trade flow for CHF); mild net energy importer but pharma-services export mix is largely oil-insensitive; modest terms-of-trade sensitivity to commodity cycles
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1: Oil direction. Oil rising → negative for Singapore (net importer, higher import bill) vs mildly negative for Switzerland (also imports energy but pharma-services buffer) → net marginally bearish SGD/CHF when oil rises. Step 2: Gold direction. Gold at $4,719/oz is elevated. Switzerland refines 65-70% of global gold, generating large trade flows when gold prices are high → positive for CHF trade balance → bearish SGD/CHF when gold is elevated. Step 3: Electronics/semiconductors. Singapore NODX electronics +74% y/y is SGD-positive but this is not a commodity driver per se — it's a manufactured goods tailwind that partially offsets commodity headwinds for SGD. Step 4: Net effect in current environment (elevated oil + elevated gold). Both directions marginally favour CHF over SGD, making the current commodity configuration a slight tactical headwind for SGD/CHF. The NODX electronics boom is the offsetting factor preventing a stronger negative commodity signal.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10% (to ~$112/bbl): Singapore terms of trade deteriorate further (higher import bill, pressure on C/A); Switzerland's pharma-services buffer limits damage; CHF safe-haven demand also rises on geopolitical risk embedded in oil spike. Net: bearish SGD/CHF by approximately -0.5% to -1.0%
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% (to ~$92/bbl): Singapore terms of trade improve (lower import bill, C/A surplus widens); Switzerland broadly unaffected; safe-haven CHF demand recedes as geopolitical risk premium compresses. Net: mildly bullish SGD/CHF by approximately +0.3% to +0.7%
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Gold is the dominant commodity driver for this pair because Switzerland's 65-70% global gold refining share creates a direct positive transmission from elevated gold prices to the Swiss trade balance and CHF demand, while Singapore has no equivalent gold exposure. At $4,719/oz gold, this creates a structural CHF tailwind that partially offsets the SGD carry and C/A surplus advantages.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The guidance divergence is clear: MAS delivered an actual policy tightening on April 14 with explicitly hawkish forward guidance ('appropriate position to respond effectively to any risk to medium-term price stability'), while the SNB's March 19 MPA introduced intervention escalation language that caps CHF strength ('willingness to intervene has increased'). For the pair, this creates a bullish SGD/CHF signal: MAS guidance pushes SGD stronger, while SNB guidance limits CHF's ability to appreciate further. The SNB's guidance is specifically CHF-bearish (intervention threat) while MAS's guidance is specifically SGD-bullish (slope tightening commitment). Both central banks enjoy high credibility — MAS's 45-year managed-float track record and the SNB's post-2015 intervention framework are well-established. The credibility differential is minimal; the DIRECTION of guidance diverges in SGD/CHF's favour.
key_bilateral_metrics: MAS April 14 tightening: slope steepened (hawkish action + guidance)
SNB March 19 hold: intervention rhetoric escalated (caps CHF strength)
Next MAS event: July 14 MPS (tightening tail ~35%)
Next SNB event: June 18 MPA (hold consensus; cut tail ~15%)
MAS credibility: among highest in G10+SGD; SNB credibility: high but tested
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has fully priced the April MAS tightening and the March SNB hold. The pair-specific edge is in the asymmetry: MAS could tighten further (SGD/CHF upside tail), while the SNB could cut to negative (also SGD/CHF upside tail) — both tails move the pair in the same direction.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, the guidance divergence compounds: MAS has committed to maintaining the steeper S$NEER slope for 'the next few quarters' (through October 2026 minimum), while the SNB's September MPA introduces the first realistic window for a negative-rate signal if medium-term inflation underperforms. The MAS October 2026 semi-annual MPS will confirm or extend the tightening commitment. The cumulative effect of two MAS MPS reviews (July, October) versus two SNB MPAs (June, September) creates multiple windows for the guidance differential to widen in SGD's favour. The US Treasury currency-manipulator monitoring constrains the SNB's intervention capacity (Switzerland meets 2 of 3 criteria; Singapore meets 2 of 3 via different criteria), creating a level playing field on intervention limits.
key_bilateral_metrics: MAS guidance horizon: steeper slope through at least October 2026
SNB guidance horizon: 0% rate + intervention rhetoric; negative rates tail
MAS October MPS: semi-annual with full Macroeconomic Review
SNB September MPA: conditional inflation forecast update (key for negative rates)
US Treasury monitoring: both countries under scrutiny for FX intervention
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices MAS holding steady and SNB on hold. We see an asymmetric probability distribution where both central bank tails (MAS tightening, SNB cutting) independently push SGD/CHF higher.

factors.horizon_split: Both bullish; strategic confidence higher (72 vs 68) because the 6M window includes multiple central bank meeting catalysts that could widen the guidance divergence.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The dominant tactical event risk — Iran ceasefire fragility — is asymmetrically bearish for SGD/CHF. Re-escalation drives CHF safe-haven demand more than SGD safe-haven demand because CHF has a higher global safe-haven beta. A ceasefire breakdown could push SGD/CHF toward 0.5950-0.6050 (150-185 pips of downside) within days, while a confirmed ceasefire would support SGD/CHF toward 0.6200-0.6250 (65-115 pips upside). The payoff distribution is negatively skewed for the pair. The June FOMC (16-17 June) is a secondary risk that affects both currencies through the USD vehicle channel but with similar magnitude. Singapore-specific events (April CPI May 25) are pair-bullish catalysts.
key_bilateral_metrics: Iran ceasefire probability: 45-55% durable; 25% re-escalation
SGD/CHF downside on re-escalation: -150 to -185 pips (to 0.5950-0.6050)
SGD/CHF upside on durable peace: +65 to +115 pips (to 0.6200-0.6250)
Payoff skew: ~1.5:1 downside vs upside (bearish asymmetry)
Singapore April CPI (May 25): bullish SGD/CHF catalyst
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price durable ceasefire at ~55% — broadly consistent with our 45-55% estimate. The pair-specific mispricing is in the asymmetric downside magnitude: CHF safe-haven demand amplifies SGD/CHF downside more than SGD benefits from ceasefire resolution.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, multiple geopolitical events create two-way risk. Iran resolution would remove CHF's main safe-haven catalyst but other flashpoints (US-China, European fiscal stress, Taiwan) could re-engage CHF demand. Singapore faces moderate event risk from US-China tariff escalation (affecting NODX channel) and China growth uncertainty. Both Singapore and Switzerland are politically stable with no domestic political risks on the horizon. Net: balanced two-way risk hence neutral.
key_bilateral_metrics: Iran resolution probability (6M): 40% peace, 35% frozen, 25% re-escalation
US-China tariff risk: moderate impact on Singapore via NODX channel
Taiwan-China tail risk: 10% over 6M (affects SGD more than CHF)
European fiscal stress: 15-20% probability (affects CHF via EUR/CHF)
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic options price balanced risks for both currencies. No clear pair-specific mispricing.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bearish (58) / Strategic Neutral (50) — the Iran ceasefire binary creates a near-term bearish asymmetry for the pair, while the strategic horizon averages across multiple events.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Both AAA; factor is non-differentiating for SGD/CHF — reduce tactical weight from 0.04 to 0.01 and strategic weight from 0.07 to 0.04

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both Singapore and Switzerland hold AAA ratings from all major agencies — the only two non-Nordic economies with universal AAA in the G10+SGD set. Singapore's federal debt-to-GDP (headline ~130% but all issued for asset management, not deficit financing; net assets deeply positive) and Switzerland's federal debt-to-GDP at 16.1% both represent pristine fiscal positions. The fiscal differential is effectively zero — both are the gold standard. Neither sovereign faces any rating action risk, no fiscal policy events in the 2-4 week window, and both benefit from constitutional/structural fiscal anchors (Singapore's President's reserve custodianship; Switzerland's debt brake since 2003). This factor generates no directional signal for SGD/CHF because both currencies share the top fiscal quality tier.
key_bilateral_metrics: Singapore: AAA (S&P, Moody's, Fitch, DBRS) — all Stable
Switzerland: AAA (S&P, Moody's, Fitch, DBRS) — all Stable
Singapore fiscal balance: surplus (structural via NIRC)
Switzerland fiscal balance: +CHF 0.3bn surplus (2025)
Both have constitutional fiscal rules
tactical.what_is_priced: The AAA-premium is identically priced for both currencies. No tactical mispricing.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, neither sovereign's fiscal trajectory changes over 6 months. Both remain the best-in-class globally. The marginal differential — Singapore's combined reserves (GIC + Temasek + MAS > SGD 2.5 trillion) are larger relative to GDP than Switzerland's (SNB + government > CHF 900bn) — is a structural positive for SGD but has been embedded in pricing for decades. The factor contributes zero directional alpha for SGD/CHF.
key_bilateral_metrics: Singapore combined reserves: > SGD 2.5 trillion
Switzerland combined reserves: > CHF 900bn
Singapore CDS: estimated ≤10bp; Switzerland CDS: estimated ≤10-15bp
Both countries balanced budgets with fiscal rules

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Neutral at high confidence (75). This factor is a non-differentiator for SGD/CHF.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The pair's trend regime is ambiguous. Spot at 0.6135 sits below the 50d SMA (0.6170), marginally below the 200d SMA (0.6154), and essentially at the 100d SMA (0.6140). The MAs are clustered within a 30-pip range, indicating a consolidation regime rather than a trending market. TradingView rates SGD/CHF as a 'strong sell' on moving averages, reflecting the below-MA spot position, but oscillator readings lean toward buy signals, suggesting the pair may be oversold within its range. Key technical levels: support at 0.6033 (52-week low), 0.6060-0.6075 (Feb consolidation floor), 0.6100 (psychological + recent pivot); resistance at 0.6170 (50d SMA), 0.6220-0.6240 (March/April highs), 0.6300 (psychological + late-April peak). The pair's estimated realised volatility of ~4.5-5.0% is low, consistent with the managed-float nature of both currencies limiting daily ranges. The MA tangle and low volatility argue for range-bound consolidation over 2-4 weeks, hence Neutral.
key_bilateral_metrics: 52-week range: 0.6033-0.6700; range percentile: 15th
Estimated RV (20d): ~4.5-5.0%
Estimated pair IV (1M): ~5.5-6.5%
tactical.what_is_priced: The option market prices a modest event premium (IV > RV) for the SNB June 18 and MAS July 14 meetings. No significant backwardation or dislocation detected.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the market structure signal is subordinate to fundamentals. The 200d SMA at 0.6154 is declining slowly, reflecting the gradual multi-quarter compression in SGD/CHF. A sustained break above 0.6200 would confirm a trend reversal; a break below 0.6033 would confirm trend continuation lower. The pair's low volatility makes breakout strategies less attractive than range-trading or carry-collection approaches.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M range expectation: 0.5950-0.6400
200d SMA trajectory: declining (bearish structural context)
Vol regime: low-vol consolidation
strategic.what_is_priced: No significant strategic mispricing in market structure.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Neutral (55/50). Market structure is a non-differentiator — the pair is range-bound with no clear trend signal.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: SGD/CHF exhibits low implied volatility (~5.5-6.5% 1M IV) modestly above realised volatility (~4.5-5.0%), embedding a small but non-trivial event premium for the SNB June 18 MPA and MAS July 14 MPS. The pair's managed-float nature on both legs structurally compresses daily ranges and volatility relative to free-floating crosses.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: Given the pair's low volatility (~5.5-6.5% IV) and the +115bps carry advantage, the directional bullish view is best expressed via spot rather than options. Options are modestly expensive relative to realised vol, and the carry advantage compounds more efficiently in spot. For binary event expression (Iran ceasefire, SNB June 18), short-dated SGD/CHF straddles could capture event-driven vol spikes.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: Key event windows: Singapore April CPI (May 25), SNB June 18 MPA (cut tail ~15%), MAS July 14 MPS (tightening tail ~25%). Spot positions should be sized to survive episodic Iran-headline spikes. Option buyers face unfavourable carry dynamics — the IV/RV premium means buying optionality is expensive relative to fundamental fair value.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: SGD/CHF is a thin cross pair. Direct liquidity is limited; most SGD/CHF transactions route through USD vehicle legs (USD/SGD × USD/CHF). Execution costs are higher with wider bid-offer spreads (~5-8 pips vs 1-2 pips for major pairs). Gap risk is elevated during low-liquidity periods. Position sizing should account for wider spreads. Cross-currency expressions may be more efficient: long SGD/JPY (positive carry + BoJ normalisation) or short EUR/SGD (ECB cut + MAS tightening) offer cleaner directional signals with higher volatility and better liquidity.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Non-USD cross; USD vehicle channel is second-order for SGD/CHF pair dynamics — reduce tactical weight from 0.11 to 0.08 and strategic weight from 0.12 to 0.09

tactical.bilateral_analysis: For SGD/CHF as a non-USD cross, USD liquidity operates through the vehicle currency channel — most FX trades settle in USD, so dollar funding conditions affect both legs. Fed at 3.75% with a hold expected at June FOMC means USD conditions are stable. Both SGD and CHF benefit similarly from USD easing (dollar smile bottom = both non-USD currencies strengthen vs USD). The differential effect is small: if USD weakens broadly, SGD/CHF is largely unaffected because both currencies gain similarly against USD. The more relevant channel is the cross-currency basis: the SGD/CHF cross-currency basis is not directly observable but can be inferred from USD/SGD and USD/CHF bases — both are moderate, non-stressed, suggesting no funding dislocation. The dollar smile framework places USD at the 'mild Goldilocks' bottom — this is broadly positive for non-USD currencies but does not discriminate between SGD and CHF.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed Funds: 3.75% (held April 29)
USD/SGD correlation to DXY: +0.60; USD/CHF correlation to DXY: +0.50-0.60
Net SGD/CHF sensitivity to DXY: minimal (~0.05 correlation)
Cross-currency basis: non-stressed for both legs
Dollar smile position: Goldilocks bottom (broadly USD-weak)
tactical.what_is_priced: USD liquidity is a wash for SGD/CHF. No bilateral mispricing through the USD channel.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the Warsh-Fed transition likely delivers easier USD policy, which benefits both SGD and CHF similarly in their USD pairs. The relative effect on SGD/CHF is minimal because the USD weakening transmits proportionally to both legs. The one differential: MAS's S$NEER appreciation slope mechanically outperforms the SNB's intervention-capped CHF when both currencies gain against USD — a second-derivative effect favouring SGD/CHF by approximately 0.5-1.0% over 6 months.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed path: 1 cut by Q4 2026 (high probability under Warsh)
S$NEER slope: ~1.5% annualised appreciation (post-tightening)
SNB intervention cap: constrains CHF appreciation pace
Net differential via USD channel: ~0.5-1.0% SGD/CHF over 6M
strategic.what_is_priced: The small differential effect via the USD channel is not explicitly priced in SGD/CHF forwards. This provides a marginal edge but at low confidence.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Neutral (55). USD liquidity is a second-order factor for this cross pair.

Non-USD cross; USD vehicle channel is second-order for SGD/CHF pair dynamics

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score of +0.240 maps to Conviction 3/5 — a clear bullish signal supported by the three highest-weighted factors (Policy +0.173, Inflation +0.126, Guidance +0.048) totalling +0.347 of positive contribution, partially offset by Risk Appetite (-0.078) and Event Risk (-0.029). The +0.240 score sits above the 0.20 threshold for conviction 3 but below the 0.40 threshold for conviction 4.
tactical.rationale: The tactical bullish case for SGD/CHF rests on three reinforcing pillars. First, the MAS-SNB policy divergence (+115bps carry differential, MAS tightening vs SNB anchored at zero) is the single strongest bilateral signal, contributing +0.173 of the +0.240 net score at the dominant 0.24 weight. Second, Singapore's +380bps GDP growth advantage and accelerating AI-electronics exports create a fundamentals gap that justifies continued SGD strength relative to Switzerland's modest 1% growth. Third, the MAS forward guidance explicitly commits to maintaining the steeper S$NEER slope while the SNB's intervention rhetoric caps CHF appreciation potential — a dual asymmetry that mechanically favours SGD/CHF. The primary offsetting force is Iran ceasefire fragility, which creates a fat left tail for SGD/CHF through the CHF safe-haven channel; together, Risk Appetite and Event Risk contribute -0.107, significantly reducing but not negating the bullish signal. The interaction of dossier views is interesting: both dossiers are bullish their respective currencies, but the SGD bullish case (conviction 3/5 tactical, 4/5 strategic) is materially stronger than the CHF bullish case (conviction 2/5 tactical, 3/5 strategic), creating a net pair signal of SGD outperformance. The bilateral carry advantage (+115bps) is fully additive because SGD is carry-positive vs CHF. The path of least resistance is a slow grind higher toward 0.6170-0.6200 over 2-4 weeks, interrupted by episodic Iran-driven volatility. The Flows factor (neutral, 0.10 weight) adds no directional signal near-term but the embedded CHF extreme short creates binary tail risk that must be managed. The External Balance factor (neutral, 0.06 weight) creates no near-term signal as current oil and gold dynamics marginally offset the structural C/A surplus advantage. Overall, the tactical picture is moderately bullish at conviction 3 with a key caveat: Iran ceasefire fragility constrains position sizing and skews the payoff distribution asymmetrically to the downside.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: SGD/CHF ranks as a moderate-conviction long among SGD crosses — below SGD/JPY (cleaner carry + BoJ normalisation) but above SGD/EUR and SGD/GBP. Among CHF shorts (expressed as longs of X/CHF), SGD/CHF ranks mid-pack — less attractive than AUD/CHF or NOK/CHF on pure carry but more attractive on fundamental quality and risk-adjusted basis given Singapore's AAA fiscal + MAS tightening combination.

MAS on hold (no July MPS communication in the 2-4 week window)
SNB rhetoric unchanged ahead of June 18 MPA
Singapore April CPI prints in line with consensus at ~1.9%
base_case.path: Slow grind from 0.6135 toward 0.6170-0.6180, testing the 50d SMA cluster in weeks 1-2. Carry accrues +10 pips. A firm Singapore April CPI print (May 25) could push to 0.6200; a soft print sees consolidation at 0.6130-0.6160.

Singapore April CPI prints 2.1%+ raising MAS July tightening odds materially
CHF weakens on safe-haven unwind as geopolitical risk premium compresses

tactical.path_expectation: The base-case path is a slow grind from 0.6135 toward 0.6170-0.6180 over weeks 1-2 as the pair tests the 50d SMA cluster, followed by a binary resolution around the Singapore April CPI release (May 25). A firm CPI print (≥2.0%) would push the pair through 0.6200; a soft print (<1.6%) would see consolidation around 0.6130-0.6160. The daily range is expected to remain narrow at 15-20 pips with episodic spikes of 40-60 pips on Iran headlines. Pullback levels: any dip below 0.6075 is an opportunity to add longs given the structural carry advantage. Volatility expected to remain low (RV ~5%) with episodic spikes on Iran headlines. The pair is unlikely to make a sustained directional move before the SNB June 18 meeting and MAS July 14 MPS provide the next pair of catalysts.

increase_conditions: Singapore April CPI ≥2.0% AND Iran ceasefire formalised AND SGD/CHF breaks above 0.6200 with sustained volume
MAS tightens further at July MPS AND SNB cuts to negative rates at June or September MPA
decrease_conditions: Iran re-escalation with Brent $110 sustained for 3+ days pushing SGD/CHF below 0.6033

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Mildly negative — downside magnitude exceeds base case payoff. Carry (+10 pips/month) improves the EV but position sizing should be conservative given the fat left tail from Iran re-escalation.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score of +0.465 maps to Conviction 3/5 — a solid bullish signal above the +0.40 threshold for conviction 3 but below the +0.65 threshold for conviction 4. Five of ten factors are bullish (Policy, Growth, Flows, External Balance, Guidance), five are neutral, and zero are bearish. This uniformly non-bearish distribution is distinctive and supports the directional call.
strategic.rationale: The strategic SGD/CHF bull case is anchored by the convergence of five structural factors. Policy & Rates (+0.180) dominates: the MAS-SNB policy divergence is on a trajectory to widen as MAS holds or tightens while the SNB stays at 0% or potentially cuts. External Balance (+0.104) is the second strongest contributor, reflecting Singapore's 17.5% C/A surplus — 2.5x Switzerland's 7% — with the AI-electronics tailwind accelerating Singapore's export earnings. Guidance & Credibility (+0.086) compounds the Policy signal: MAS has committed to steeper appreciation for multiple quarters, while the SNB is rhetorically capped. The factor interactions reinforce cleanly: MAS tightening → SGD appreciation → Singapore import-deflation → MAS holds or tightens further → positive feedback loop for SGD. Concurrently, Switzerland's 0.6% CPI may undershoot further as CHF strength transmits to producer prices (-2.7% PPI/IPI y/y already), potentially forcing SNB toward negative rates — which independently pushes SGD/CHF higher. The both-dossier-bullish configuration is partially offsetting at the pair level, but the SGD dossier's strategic conviction (4/5, score +0.659) materially exceeds the CHF dossier's (3/5, score +0.237), creating a clear net signal for SGD/CHF upside. The path of least resistance over 6 months is gradual SGD/CHF appreciation toward 0.6300-0.6400, with the carry accrual adding approximately 60 pips of return. Inflation & Growth (+0.062) and Flows (+0.033) provide supporting contributions, with the structural Swiss FDI flows into Singapore and Singapore's family office inflows providing a persistent SGD bid that compounds over the strategic horizon. The five neutral factors (Risk Appetite, Event Risk, Fiscal & Sovereign, Market Structure, USD Liquidity) contribute zero drag — distinguishing this from the tactical picture where Risk Appetite and Event Risk created active headwinds. The strategic case is cleaner and more directional than the tactical case precisely because the Iran binary risk diminishes in relevance over a 6-month horizon.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: SGD/CHF ranks as a mid-tier strategic long — the carry is modest but positive, the fundamental differential is clear, and the risk profile is contained by both currencies' managed-float/safe-haven characteristics. Among SGD crosses, it ranks below SGD/JPY and short USD/SGD but above SGD/EUR. Among CHF shorts, it is one of the better risk-adjusted options given SGD's AAA quality reduces counterparty risk and MAS tightening provides a durable fundamental catalyst.

SNB holds at 0% at June and September MPAs
Fed cuts once under Warsh by Q4 2026
Iran partially resolves; oil settles $80-90 by Q3 2026
Singapore NODX sustains +8-12% y/y through year-end
base_case.path: SGD/CHF consolidates 0.6100-0.6200 through May-June, testing the MA cluster. Singapore CPI releases and SNB June 18 provide the first catalytic window. Pair breaks above 0.6200 in July around MAS MPS if MAS-supportive data compounds. Acceleration toward 0.6300+ in Q3 as Fed cuts and oil normalises. Staircase-higher pattern with consolidation phases between each central bank catalyst.

SNB cuts to -0.25% at June or September MPA (15% probability)
Oil normalises to $75 by Q3; Singapore NODX sustains +12% y/y
Iran re-escalation sustained for 4+ weeks with Brent above $115
China hard landing hitting Singapore NODX (Caixin PMI <48 sustained)

strategic.path_expectation: SGD/CHF consolidates in the 0.6100-0.6200 range through May-June, testing the 50d/200d MA cluster at 0.6154-0.6170. The Singapore April/May CPI releases (May 25, June 23) and the SNB June 18 MPA provide the first catalytic window — a hold at 0% from the SNB combined with elevated Singapore CPI sets up the July 14 MAS MPS as the key event. If MAS-supportive data compounds and the SNB holds at 0% (base case), the pair breaks above 0.6200 in July and establishes a new range. Acceleration toward 0.6300+ occurs in Q3 2026 if the Fed cuts and oil normalises to the $80-90 range, removing Singapore's energy import headwind and compressing CHF's safe-haven premium. The pattern shape is staircase-higher with consolidation phases of 2-4 weeks between each central bank catalyst. Pullback levels for adding long SGD/CHF: 0.6050-0.6100. The carry accrual of ~60 pips over 6 months provides a cushion and means the pair can deliver positive total returns even if spot appreciation is modest.

MAS tightens further AND SNB cuts to -0.25% AND Iran resolves durably with oil returning to $80-85

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Mildly positive — upside scenarios deliver more than downside takes, and carry (+60 pips at 6M) improves all scenarios by ~0.98% total return, making the probability-weighted EV clearly positive.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: CoinCodex forecasts SGD/CHF ranging 0.5814-0.6145 for 2026 with an average of 0.5978 (decidedly bearish); 30rates.com forecasts SGD/CHF at 0.640 for end-May, declining to 0.631 by end-June and 0.621 by end-November
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Bullish — SGD/CHF target 0.6175 (4-week) with upside to 0.6250-0.6300 on ceasefire/CPI catalysts
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Bullish — SGD/CHF target 0.6300 by end-October; 0.6250-0.6350 base case range by November 2026
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: above consensus (30rates.com) and well above CoinCodex algorithmic model
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: +80 pips above 30rates.com October forecast of 0.623; well above CoinCodex average forecast of 0.5978 by approximately +320 pips
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We differ from consensus on four key dimensions: (1) algorithmic models underweight the MAS April 2026 policy tightening impulse — this is a structural S$NEER slope change that mechanically delivers SGD appreciation and is not captured in backward-looking technical models; (2) consensus under-prices the AI-electronics export tailwind as a durable SGD-specific positive — the +74% y/y March electronics NODX print reflects a structural supercycle, not a one-off; (3) the +115bps carry advantage compounds systematically in SGD's favour over time and is not properly reflected in algorithmic price projections that extrapolate recent downtrends; (4) the SNB negative-rates tail risk (15-25% probability) is significantly under-priced by consensus, creating an asymmetric upside scenario for SGD/CHF that has limited downside counterpart.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: +2.7% spot appreciation + 60 pips carry = ~3.7% total return over 6 months
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: Spot flat to mildly lower (consistent with CoinCodex average 0.5978), partially offset by carry accrual of ~60 pips — limited net downside if consensus is correct

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Both horizons are directionally aligned at conviction 3/5 Bullish. The strategic weighted score is materially higher than tactical (+0.465 vs +0.240) because External Balance weight rises from 0.06 to 0.16 and Guidance weight rises from 0.07 to 0.12, both bullish factors, while the tactical window's Iran-driven headwinds from Risk Appetite and Event Risk (combined -0.107 drag) largely wash out over the strategic horizon as risk sentiment averages across multiple episodes.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Build a long SGD/CHF core position now, add on any dips below 0.6075, expect cumulative +2.5-3.5% over 6 months with carry. No horizon flip is anticipated — the thesis strengthens monotonically as carry accrues and catalysts resolve in the MAS-tightening / SNB-on-hold direction.

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: ~0.6141 (+6 pips; reflecting +115bps carry in SGD's favour)

catalysts.rationale: Consensus expects 1.9-2.0% headline; oil pass-through creates upside risk to 2.1%. A core print ≥1.8% lifts MAS July tightening odds meaningfully, compressing USD/SGD and supporting SGD/CHF by 15-30 pips via the S$NEER appreciation channel. A second consecutive elevated print would lock in MAS tightening thesis for July MPS review, providing a durable SGD-positive signal for the pair.

catalysts.event: China May CPI and Trade Data
catalysts.rationale: Singapore is highly China-exposed via the NODX channel — approximately 12-15% of Singapore non-oil domestic exports go to China. Strong China data strengthens the SGD growth narrative and supports SGD/CHF via improved export earnings; weak China data undermines the Singapore AI-electronics tailwind thesis, bearish for SGD/CHF. CHF is significantly less China-sensitive given Switzerland's domestic services and pharma orientation, making this catalyst primarily a base-leg (SGD) event with asymmetric bilateral transmission.

catalysts.event: June FOMC (Powell final / Warsh handover period)
catalysts.rationale: Affects both SGD and CHF similarly through the USD vehicle currency channel. Dovish Fed guidance or a cut marginally favours SGD/CHF via the S$NEER slope mechanism, which captures USD weakness more efficiently than the SNB's intervention-capped CHF, giving SGD a slight incremental benefit. However, the net bilateral impact is near-zero as both currencies appreciate against USD in a dovish Fed environment, making this a pair-neutral event with a marginal SGD edge only in tail dovish scenarios.

BoJ rate decision primarily affects JPY, with indirect transmission to SGD/CHF through cross-currency rebalancing via SGD/JPY and CHF/JPY pairs. A BoJ hike would strengthen JPY, potentially triggering safe-haven competition dynamics between JPY and CHF, modestly reducing CHF's safe-haven premium and thus marginally bullish for SGD/CHF. However, the bilateral transmission to SGD/CHF is indirect and second-order, making this a neutral event for the pair specifically.

Consensus expects hold at 0.00%. A hold with maintained intervention rhetoric is mildly CHF-bearish (bullish SGD/CHF) as it confirms the SNB is not tightening and intervention threats cap CHF upside. A cut to -0.25% (15% tail probability) would be strongly bullish SGD/CHF by an estimated +100-150 pips as negative rates reduce the attractiveness of CHF safe-haven positioning and widen the SGD/CHF rate differential significantly. Only a hawkish inflation surprise leading to SNB tightening bias (~5-10% probability) would be bearish SGD/CHF. The asymmetry of outcomes makes this event net bullish for SGD/CHF at the probability-weighted level.

Second consecutive elevated Singapore CPI print would decisively lock in the MAS tightening thesis for the July 14 MPS review. A core print ≥1.8% for two consecutive months creates near-certainty of MAS maintaining or extending the steeper S$NEER slope, which is the primary structural SGD-bullish mechanism for the pair. This catalyst compounds the May 25 CPI signal — if both prints are firm, MAS July tightening probability would likely rise from 25% to 40%+, providing SGD/CHF with a clean fundamental tailwind into the July MPS.

catalysts.event: MAS July 2026 Quarterly Monetary Policy Statement
catalysts.rationale: The most important pair-specific catalyst in the strategic window. A hold (75% probability) maintains the steeper S$NEER slope status quo and confirms the April tightening is durable — neutral-to-mildly bullish for SGD/CHF as the carry and appreciation slope persist. A further tightening (25% probability) would push SGD/CHF toward 0.6300+ as it confirms an accelerating MAS tightening cycle that further widens the bilateral policy differential against the SNB-at-zero base. A dovish reversal (5% probability) would be a sharp SGD/CHF sell signal and invalidate the thesis. The probability-weighted outcome is bullish.

By September, Iran resolution and summer CPI prints determine whether the SNB signals movement toward negative rates. If the SNB's conditional medium-term inflation forecast drops below 0.3% (core Swiss CPI currently at 0.3% and declining), a cut to -0.25% becomes the base case for December — the signal at September MPA would be strongly bullish for SGD/CHF by +100-150 pips. A hold at 0% with increasingly cautious inflation language is mildly bullish. Only a surprise hawkish shift (near-zero probability absent a dramatic upside CPI development) would be bearish SGD/CHF. The 25-40% probability of negative-rate signalling makes this the highest-impact event risk in the strategic window.

invalidators.condition: Iran ceasefire collapses with Brent sustained above $115/bbl for 5+ consecutive trading days
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral — carry advantage overwhelmed by CHF safe-haven bid; thesis suspended pending de-escalation
invalidators.mechanism: A sustained oil spike above $115 simultaneously: (1) drives CHF safe-haven demand as geopolitical risk premium surges, overwhelming the +115bps carry advantage; (2) deteriorates Singapore's terms of trade as a net oil importer; (3) triggers CHF asset-manager short-covering at extreme levels (-37,534 contracts, 95th percentile), creating a mechanical amplifier that pushes SGD/CHF below 0.6000. The combination of CHF safe-haven demand, Singapore ToT deterioration, and CHF short-squeeze creates a convergent bearish pressure that outweighs all fundamental SGD positives.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Brent crude front-month futures + Iran peace process news flow

invalidators.condition: MAS dovishly reverses the April 2026 slope tightening at July 14 MPS (flattening the S$NEER slope back to pre-April level)
invalidators.new_stance: Bearish — the primary SGD-bullish catalyst is invalidated while CHF structural supports remain intact
invalidators.mechanism: MAS reversing the April tightening would signal that Singapore's growth and inflation outlook has deteriorated beyond expectations, removing the policy divergence that drives the carry, guidance, and growth factors simultaneously. All three bullish factors that contribute the most weighted score (+0.173, +0.126, +0.048) would flip to neutral or bearish, collapsing the net score from +0.240 to near-zero or negative. SGD/CHF would likely fall to the 0.5900-0.6000 range as the fundamental SGD thesis is invalidated.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: MAS Monetary Policy Statement (July 14 and October)
invalidators.monitor_frequency: Semi-annual (MPS) with monthly NODX data as leading indicator

invalidators.condition: SNB cuts to -0.25% AND simultaneously announces CHF 100bn/quarter FX intervention programme
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral — combined policy response creates offsetting forces with uncertain near-term trajectory
invalidators.mechanism: While a rate cut to negative independently pushes SGD/CHF higher, a simultaneous announcement of massive FX intervention (CHF 100bn/quarter = ~$110bn) would overwhelm the rate signal in the near term. Historical precedent (SNB's 2011 EUR/CHF floor, 2015 removal) shows that large intervention programmes compress cross-pairs before rate effects dominate. The near-term intervention force could keep SGD/CHF range-bound despite the rate cut signal, creating a Neutral rather than Bullish outcome until the intervention programme's trajectory becomes clear.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SNB sight deposits (weekly) + SNB MPA communications + EUR/CHF dynamics

invalidators.condition: Singapore Q2 2026 NODX growth falls below +3% y/y on a 3-month moving average basis
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral — AI-electronics tailwind invalidated, reducing the bilateral growth differential to near-zero
invalidators.mechanism: Singapore's NODX growth (currently +15.3% y/y in March, electronics +74%) is the empirical foundation of the +380bps GDP differential that drives the Inflation & Growth factor (weighted 0.18 tactical). A collapse to +3% would eliminate Singapore's growth premium versus Switzerland, reducing the Inflation & Growth contribution from +0.126 to near-zero and removing the MAS tightening rationale (below-target growth → MAS pivot). The net weighted score would fall from +0.240 to approximately +0.110, insufficient for conviction 3 bullish.

invalidators.condition: China hard landing materialises: Caixin Manufacturing PMI below 48 for 2 consecutive months AND USD/CNH breaks above 7.40
invalidators.new_stance: Bearish — Singapore's largest non-oil trade exposure craters, overwhelming all carry and policy advantages
invalidators.mechanism: Singapore's economy has disproportionate exposure to China through the NODX channel (12-15% of NODX to China) and the regional financial hub channel (Chinese family offices, wealth flows). A China hard landing combined with CNH depreciation would: (1) crash Singapore electronics/machinery exports to China; (2) trigger regional risk-off, strengthening CHF's global safe-haven status; (3) likely force MTI to revise Singapore GDP forecast below 2.0%, reducing MAS tightening probability toward zero; (4) potentially trigger MAS S$NEER slope reversal. The combined effect would flip the pair's top three factors (Policy, Growth, Guidance) from bullish to neutral or bearish, turning the thesis from Bullish to Bearish.

signposts.confirms_if: Settles into $75-95 range over Q3 2026 — energy normalisation improves Singapore's terms of trade, reduces CHF safe-haven demand, and supports SGD/CHF upside thesis
signposts.invalidates_if: Breaks above $115/bbl sustained for 5+ trading days — triggers CHF safe-haven surge and deteriorates Singapore ToT
signposts.bilateral_importance: Both currencies are net energy importers but CHF uniquely benefits from safe-haven demand during oil spikes driven by geopolitical risk premiums. Singapore additionally faces direct terms-of-trade deterioration as a major oil importer and refiner, making oil the single most important external variable for SGD/CHF directional risk.

signposts.confirms_if: Core sustains 1.7-2.5% through Q3 2026 — confirms MAS tightening case intact and provides justification for July MPS slope maintenance or extension
signposts.invalidates_if: Core falls below 1.0% for 2+ consecutive months — signals domestic demand weakness that would trigger MAS dovish pivot risk, undermining the primary SGD-bullish thesis
signposts.bilateral_importance: Singapore CPI is the direct input into MAS's S$NEER slope decision mechanism — the most important policy lever affecting SGD/CHF. Unlike most G10 pairs where CPI affects rates with a 6-12 month lag, MAS uses CPI directly to calibrate the S$NEER slope, making Singapore CPI uniquely high-impact for this specific pair.

signposts.confirms_if: Headline stays 0.3-0.8% — real-yield case for CHF intact but provides no additional tightening signal, keeping SNB anchored at 0% as the base case
signposts.invalidates_if: Core rises above 1.0% for 2+ consecutive months — would shift SNB from neutral to hawkish bias, lifting CHF and potentially pushing SGD/CHF lower via the guidance and policy factors
signposts.bilateral_importance: Swiss CPI determines the SNB's negative-rate calculus — the tail event that would most amplify the bilateral policy differential in SGD's favour. A core undershoot below 0.2% accelerates the probability of negative rates at June or September MPA, which is the highest-magnitude upside scenario for SGD/CHF. Monitoring Swiss CPI for signs of persistent disinflation is essential for assessing this tail.

signposts.confirms_if: Reserves continue rising at USD 5-10bn/month — confirms MAS is leaning against SGD strength (near S$NEER band ceiling) but not actively weakening SGD; the slow reserve accumulation is consistent with MAS allowing SGD appreciation within the steeper slope
signposts.invalidates_if: Reserves fall by USD 20bn or more in a single month — signals MAS defending the S$NEER floor, indicating unexpected SGD weakness and a fundamental shift in Singapore's external position that would undermine the bullish thesis
signposts.bilateral_importance: MAS reserves are the direct operational fingerprint of S$NEER band management. For SGD/CHF, rising reserves confirm that MAS is at or near the appreciation ceiling of the band — which is the most bullish structural configuration for SGD. Falling reserves signal band stress or active SGD-weakening intervention, a key leading indicator of MAS policy reversal risk.

signposts.confirms_if: Spot breaks above 0.6170 and holds for 5+ consecutive trading days — confirms the consolidation range is resolving higher and triggers trend-following momentum flows
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot breaks below 0.6033 (52-week low) — confirms the trend remains lower and the range has resolved to the downside, signalling continuation of the CHF-strengthening regime
signposts.bilateral_importance: The MA cluster at 0.6140-0.6170 (50d/100d/200d SMA within 30 pips of each other) is the technical key for the pair. A sustained break above this cluster removes the most significant technical resistance between spot and the 0.6220-0.6240 zone (next resistance). For this managed-float pair with low volatility (~5% RV), MA breaks are meaningful signals given the slow-moving nature of the pair's technical structure.

assumptions: SGD/CHF is a thin cross pair. Direct liquidity is limited; most SGD/CHF transactions route through the USD vehicle legs (USD/SGD × USD/CHF). This means execution costs are higher (~5-8 pips bid-offer vs 1-2 pips for major pairs) and gap risk is elevated during low-liquidity periods. Position sizing should account for wider spreads.

Carry calculation proxy: The +115bps carry is estimated from SORA 3M Compounded (~1.15%) vs SNB policy rate (0.00%). Actual forward points may vary due to cross-currency basis adjustments and liquidity premiums specific to the SGD/CHF cross.

Implied volatility for SGD/CHF was not directly retrieved from public sources. The ~5.5-6.5% estimate is triangulated from USD/SGD IV (~5.0%) and EUR/CHF IV (~7-8%) adjusted for pair correlation and both currencies' managed-float characteristics.

US Treasury currency-manipulator monitoring: Both Singapore and Switzerland meet 2 of 3 US Treasury criteria (different criteria for each country), constraining both central banks' FX intervention capacity and creating an approximate level playing field on this dimension.

Weight adjustments: Risk Appetite weight boosted +3% tactical and +3% strategic to reflect the pair's elevated Iran-sensitivity through the CHF safe-haven channel. Fiscal & Sovereign weight reduced -3% tactical and -3% strategic as both are AAA (non-differentiating). USD Liquidity weight reduced -3% tactical and -3% strategic as a non-USD cross where the vehicle currency channel is second-order.

Algorithmic forecast sources (CoinCodex, 30rates.com) are based on technical/algorithmic models that do not incorporate the MAS April 2026 tightening or Iran-war macro dynamics. Their bearish bias reflects extrapolation of the prior downtrend rather than forward-looking fundamental assessment, explaining our significant divergence from their forecasts.

CFTC positioning for SGD is effectively zero (no liquid futures market), limiting the usefulness of standard positioning analysis for the base currency leg. The CHF CFTC data (-37,534 contracts, 95th percentile) is used as a proxy for the quote leg only.

Iran ceasefire probability estimates (45-55% durable, 25% re-escalation) are based on available geopolitical news flow as of May 9, 2026 and carry significant uncertainty. These probabilities directly affect the Risk Appetite and Event Risk factor assessments and their combined -0.107 tactical weighted contribution.

complete_citations.title: Switzerland-Singapore Bilateral Relations
complete_citations.publisher: Swiss Federal Department of Foreign Affairs (FDFA)
